entries.title: Week 11 Reflection: When Courts Don't Defer — Correctness Review and the Limits of Vavilov
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of March 24, 2026
This week the course returned to the Vavilov framework to examine its limits. The presumption of reasonableness review — which we covered in Week 10 — is just that: a presumption. Vavilov carved out four categories where correctness applies instead: constitutional questions, questions of central importance to the legal system outside the adjudicator's expertise, questions regarding jurisdictional boundaries between administrative bodies, and cases with a legislated standard of review. The most practically significant of these is the first. When a decision raises a genuine constitutional question — whether the decision-maker had authority to act, whether a statute conforms to the Constitution Act, whether a Charter right was engaged — courts do not defer to the decision-maker's own view. They apply their own judgment, because the question of constitutional validity cannot, by its nature, be left to the institution whose authority is in question. The week also covered Doré v Barreau du Québec (2012 SCC 12), which governs how Charter rights are treated when they are engaged not by a statute but by a discretionary administrative decision — and York Region District School Board v Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario (2024 SCC 22), which clarified that the threshold question of whether a Charter right is engaged attracts correctness, while the proportionate balancing that follows is reviewed for reasonableness.
In February 2026, the Federal Court of Appeal released Canada (Attorney General) v Canadian Civil Liberties Association, 2026 FCA 6. The case arose from the federal government's invocation of the Emergencies Act in February 2022, during the Freedom Convoy protests in Ottawa. The Federal Court had already found in 2024 that the invocation was unlawful — the statutory threshold conditions for declaring a public order emergency had not been satisfied. The federal government appealed, arguing among other things that Cabinet decisions of this nature are not reviewable by federal courts at all. The FCA rejected that argument directly: Cabinet exercises statutory powers vested by legislation, and its decisions are accordingly subject to judicial review. There is no category of Cabinet decision that is simply immune from review.
Applying the Vavilov framework, the legal analysis is relatively clean. The question of whether the Emergencies Act invocation satisfied the constitutional and statutory conditions for its exercise is a constitutional and statutory scope question — it attracts correctness review, not reasonableness. Courts do not defer to a decision-maker's view on whether it had legal authority to act in the first place. That is precisely what the correctness exception for constitutional questions is designed to address. The government's argument for non-reviewability, if accepted, would have created a constitutional accountability gap — a domain of executive action insulated from independent judicial oversight — that conflicts not only with Vavilov but with the deeper principle from Crevier that jurisdictional and constitutional questions must remain reviewable. The FCA's answer reaffirmed that the scale of the decision, or the seniority of the decision-maker, does not change the basic constitutional structure.
The York Region case adds a further layer that is relevant if the Emergencies Act invocation also engaged Charter rights. Protesters argued that restrictions on the convoy engaged their s.2 rights (freedom of expression and peaceful assembly) and potentially s.7 (security of the person). Under the York Region framework, the threshold question — whether a Charter right was engaged at all — is a correctness question. But once engagement is established, Doré takes over: the decision-maker must demonstrate that it proportionately balanced the Charter right against the statutory objective it was pursuing. That balancing is reviewed for reasonableness, not correctness. This two-stage structure matters because it determines how much deference a court owes to the government's judgment about the balance struck between emergency powers and protected rights. On the threshold question — engagement — no deference. On the proportionate weighing — deference to reasonable balancing.
The Emergencies Act litigation is the most significant public law case in recent Canadian history, and it illustrates exactly what correctness review exists to do. Emergency powers, by their nature, operate quickly and broadly, affecting large numbers of people with limited prior consultation. They are also the powers most likely to be misused, because they are invoked in conditions of political pressure and public urgency. The Vavilov correctness exception for constitutional questions ensures that when a government reaches for that kind of authority, a court will independently verify that the constitutional conditions for doing so were actually met — not accept the government's own assessment. That independent check is not hostile to administrative expertise; it is the constitutional backstop that makes broad statutory delegation of power legitimate in the first place.
Cases referenced: Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 | Doré v Barreau du Québec, 2012 SCC 12 | York Region District School Board v Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario, 2024 SCC 22 | Canada (Attorney General) v Canadian Civil Liberties Association, 2026 FCA 6 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220

---

- Canada (Attorney General) v Canadian Civil Liberties Association, 2026 FCA 6 — Analysis (Administrative Law Matters)
- York Region District School Board v ETFO, 2024 SCC 22 — New Correctness Category (Harper Grey)
- Doré v Barreau du Québec, 2012 SCC 12 (CanLII)
- Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 (CanLII)
- A floodgate of correctness? New correctness categories post-Vavilov (Dentons)

entries.title: Is AGCO an admin decision maker?
author: Fukun
entries.body:
---
# Is AGCO an Administrative Decision-Maker?
### A Weekly Admin Law Observation · Osgoode Hall · Winter 2026
TL;DR
AGCO is unambiguously an administrative decision-maker. It is created by statute, receives its powers from the Ontario legislature, exercises compulsory public authority over licensing and penalties, and its decisions are subject to judicial review. Three intuitive-sounding arguments against this conclusion all fail on first principles of administrative law.

---
## Why this question matters
If you have ever bought alcohol, visited a casino, purchased cannabis from a licensed retailer, or bet on a horse race in Ontario, your transaction was regulated by the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario (AGCO).
The AGCO touches everyday commercial life across the province. Whether it qualifies as an administrative decision-maker (ADM) is not just a classification exercise, it determines whether its decisions are subject to the full force of administrative law: the duty of procedural fairness, substantive review for reasonableness, and the possibility of judicial review at the Superior Court.
The short answer is: yes, unambiguously. But arriving at that answer requires clearing away three persistent misconceptions.

---
## 1. What is AGCO? The statutory structure
AGCO is established by s. 2 of the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario Act, 2019, SO 2019, c 15, Sched 1.
Under s. 1.1 (added by 2020 amendment), the Commission is explicitly declared an agent of the Crown in right of Ontario, firmly placing it within the executive branch of government.
Its key structural features under the Act:
In addition to the 2019 Act, AGCO administers a suite of enabling statutes, including the Liquor Licence and Control Act, 2019, the Gaming Control Act, 1992, the Cannabis Licence Act, 2018, and the Horse Racing Licence Act, 2015.

---
## 2. The administrative decision-maker test
Administrative law defines an ADM as any individual or body that has been delegated decision-making authority by the legislature through an enabling statute, and that exercises powers affecting the rights, interests, or legitimate expectations of others.
The defining feature is the source of power: delegation by the legislature, not the form of the body, its name, or how its members are appointed.
Applying this to AGCO:
- Delegated authority? Yes, ss. 2 and 3 of the AGCO Act, 2019 expressly grant regulatory and administrative powers.
- Affects rights and interests? Yes,AGCO issues, refuses, suspends, and revokes licences; imposes monetary penalties; and enforces compliance obligations with real economic consequences.
- Public in character? Yes, regulates publicly important industries (alcohol, cannabis, gaming) under compulsory provincial authority.
AGCO is an ADM. So is its Registrar. Both exercise delegated statutory powers that affect legal rights.

---
## 3. Three misconceptions — and why they fail
When examining whether AGCO is an ADM, three intuitive arguments arise. All three are wrong.
### ❌ Misconception A: "Cabinet appoints the board, so AGCO can't be an ADM"
"Only the legislature can appoint administrative decision-makers and set their salaries."
This reverses how the administrative state actually works. ADMs are part of the executive branch — and Cabinet is the executive. It is entirely normal, expected, and lawful for Cabinet to appoint board members and set their compensation. That is how virtually every tribunal, regulator, and Crown agency in Canada operates.
The legislature creates the body and delegates the power. Cabinet administers and staffs it. Neither function disqualifies the other. AGCO's Roncarelli obligations — to stay within its statutory mandate and exercise its powers in good faith — exist independently of who appoints its members.
### ❌ Misconception B: "LAT hears appeals from the Registrar, so the Registrar can't be an ADM too"
"Because the Licence Appeal Tribunal is an administrative decision-maker, the Registrar cannot also be an administrative actor."
This misunderstands how tiered administrative systems work. Canadian administrative law is full of layered structures where one ADM hears appeals from another ADM. Think of the Immigration and Refugee Board and the Refugee Appeal Division, or the WSIB and the Workplace Safety and Insurance Appeals Tribunal.
The existence of a downstream appeal body does not retroactively strip the first-instance decision-maker of its character. Both the AGCO Registrar (first-instance) and LAT (appellate) are ADMs operating at different levels of the same statutory scheme.
### ❌ Misconception C: "AGCO hasn't been delegated power by the legislature"
This is simply factually wrong. Sections 2 and 3 of the AGCO Act, 2019 expressly create the Commission and delegate regulatory authority over alcohol, cannabis, gaming, and horse racing to it. The Commission openly describes itself as a provincial regulatory agency. Calling this "not a delegation" would contradict the plain text of the statute.

---
## 4. Is AGCO subject to judicial review? Applying Wall
Even if AGCO is an ADM, we still need to ask whether its decisions are subject to judicial review (JR). The governing test comes from Highwood Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v Wall, 2018 SCC 26:
JR is available only when (1) there is an exercise of state authority, and (2) that exercise is of a sufficiently public character.
Applying Wall to AGCO:
Step 1 — State authority? Clearly yes. AGCO's powers flow directly from Ontario statutes. Its licensing decisions, monetary penalties, and enforcement actions are statutory, not contractual or voluntary. It is not a private club or self-governing professional association acting on its own rules — it is the Crown acting through delegation.
Step 2 — Sufficiently public character? Also yes. Using the contextual factors from Air Canada v Toronto Port Authority, 2011 FCA 347 (Stratas J):
- AGCO's decisions are shaped entirely by statute, not private discretion.
- It exercises compulsory powers (you cannot operate a licensed establishment without AGCO approval).
- It is directed by and accountable to the provincial government.
- Its decisions carry major public dimensions — public health, consumer protection, organized crime prevention.
Both Wall criteria are met. AGCO decisions are subject to judicial review.

---
## 5. The decision chain in practice
Understanding AGCO as an ADM has a direct practical consequence, it means affected parties have a structured legal pathway to challenge decisions:
AGCO Registrar decision (first instance)
        ↓
Licence Appeal Tribunal — LAT
(appeal within 15 days under s. 14(7) AGCO Act, 2019)
        ↓
Judicial Review — Ontario Superior Court of Justice (Divisional Court)
(on questions of law, procedural fairness, or unreasonableness)
The Licence Appeal Tribunal is an independent adjudicative tribunal under Tribunals Ontario. Its decisions on AGCO matters are in turn reviewable by the courts, with the standard of review governed by the Vavilov framework (Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65).

---
## 6. Not constitutionally independent — and that's the point
One important feature: AGCO board members' salaries are set by Cabinet, their appointments are not security-protected, and the Commission can be restructured by the legislature. This is not a deficiency — it is precisely what confirms AGCO's status as an ADM rather than a court.
Courts enjoy constitutional independence under ss. 96, 99, and 100 of the Constitution Act, 1867. ADMs do not, and are not meant to. They are executive bodies performing regulatory functions. Their accountability runs to the legislature through statute, and to the courts through judicial review — not through constitutional tenure guarantees.

---
## Key takeaways
1. The source of power is everything. An ADM is defined by legislative delegation, not by its name or organizational form.
1. Cabinet appointment is normal and expected for executive branch bodies — it doesn't undermine ADM status.
1. Tiered appeals between ADMs are common in Canadian administrative law. One appeal body does not erase the first-instance ADM.
1. AGCO passes the Wall test on both limbs: state authority and sufficiently public character.
1. AGCO decisions are judicially reviewable at the Ontario Divisional Court following LAT proceedings, applying the Vavilov reasonableness standard.

---
## Sources and further reading
### Legislation
- Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario Act, 2019, SO 2019, c 15, Sched 1
- Liquor Licence and Control Act, 2019
- Gaming Control Act, 1992
- Cannabis Licence Act, 2018
- Horse Racing Licence Act, 2015
- Licence Appeal Tribunal Act, 1999
### Key cases
- Highwood Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v Wall, 2018 SCC 26 — the threshold test for judicial review availability
- Air Canada v Toronto Port Authority, 2011 FCA 347 — 8-factor "sufficiently public character" test
- Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 1999 SCC 40 (CanLII) — leading case on procedural fairness and ADM obligations
- Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 — standard of review framework
- [Roncarelli v Duplessis, [1959] SCR 121](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1959/1959canscc1/1959canscc1.html) — ADMs must act within their statutory mandate; arbitrary exercises of power are unlawful
### Further reading
- AGCO — Who We Are
- Tribunals Ontario — Licence Appeal Tribunal
- LAT Information Sheet — AGCO Appeals

entries.title: Week 10 Reflection: The HRTO Got Its Reasoning Wrong — and the Court Said So
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of March 17, 2026
This week the course covered standard of review — the framework courts use to decide how closely to scrutinize an administrative decision when it is challenged on judicial review. The organizing case is Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, which restructured the law by establishing a presumption of reasonableness review as the default, displacing the prior Dunsmuir framework's more contested category-sorting approach. Under Vavilov, reasonableness is not satisfied by any defensible outcome — a reasonable decision must be justified, transparent, and intelligible. It must engage with the key arguments the parties actually made and trace a logical path from the evidence and the law to the conclusion reached. Correctness review is reserved for a narrow set of categories: constitutional questions, questions of central importance to the legal system outside the adjudicator's expertise, and questions regarding jurisdictional boundaries between administrative bodies. For almost everything else, courts defer — but defer to the quality of the reasoning, not to the outcome alone.
On March 17 — the first class day of standard of review week — the Ontario Divisional Court released Burland v Precise ParkLink Inc, 2026 ONSC 1587. The Court quashed an Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario decision that had dismissed a human rights application. The problem was not that the HRTO reached the wrong result — the Divisional Court did not substitute its own view of the merits. The problem was that the Tribunal's reasons were "peremptory": brief, conclusory, and devoid of the justification, transparency, and intelligibility that Vavilov requires. The matter was remitted to be decided again, by a differently constituted panel, with reasons that actually engage with the applicant's case.
Applying the Vavilov framework to what went wrong in Burland makes the analysis concrete. Vavilov requires a reviewing court to read the decision as a whole, in context, and to ask whether the reasoning holds together — not whether it is perfect, but whether it is coherent and responsive to the live issues. Peremptory reasons fail this standard in a specific way: they tell the applicant that their complaint does not succeed, but they do not explain why. The applicant receives no understanding of which factual findings the Tribunal made, which legal test it applied, or which arguments it found unpersuasive. That is not just a procedural fairness problem — it is a substantive reasonableness problem, because a decision that cannot be traced from premises to conclusion cannot be assessed as reasonable or unreasonable by a reviewing court.
This is where the procedural fairness doctrine from Week 8 and the standard of review doctrine from this week reinforce each other rather than operating independently. Baker established a context-sensitive duty to give reasons where the stakes for the individual are significant. Vavilov made the quality of those reasons the central criterion of substantive reasonableness. Together, they create a layered accountability structure: a decision-maker must give reasons (procedural fairness), and those reasons must actually justify the decision (substantive reasonableness). A decision that technically produces a reasons document but fills it with bare conclusions satisfies neither requirement.
The Vavilov framework is sometimes described as a deferential standard that keeps courts out of administrative decision-making. Burland illustrates that this description is incomplete. Vavilov defers to the decision-maker's expertise and mandate — it does not defer to inadequate reasoning. The more specialized and expert the tribunal, the more its substantive judgments should be respected; but expert institutions must show their work. An HRTO that dismisses a human rights complaint without explaining why is not being deferred to under Vavilov — it is being sent back to try again.
Cases referenced: Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 | Burland v Precise ParkLink Inc, 2026 ONSC 1587

---
Sources
- Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 (CanLII)
- Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario
- HRTO 2025 Case Law in Review — Ontario Bar Association

On March 17 — the first class day of standard of review week — the Ontario Divisional Court released

decision that had dismissed a human rights application. The problem was not that the HRTO reached the wrong result — the Divisional Court did not substitute its own view of the merits. The problem was that the Tribunal's reasons were "peremptory": brief, conclusory, and devoid of the justification, transparency, and intelligibility that
requires. The matter was remitted to be decided again, by a differently constituted panel, with reasons that actually engage with the applicant's case.
framework to what went wrong in

requires a reviewing court to read the decision as a whole, in context, and to ask whether the reasoning holds together — not whether it is perfect, but whether it is coherent and responsive to the live issues. Peremptory reasons fail this standard in a specific way: they tell the applicant that their complaint does not succeed, but they do not explain why. The applicant receives no understanding of which factual findings the Tribunal made, which legal test it applied, or which arguments it found unpersuasive. That is not just a procedural fairness problem — it is a substantive reasonableness problem, because a decision that cannot be traced from premises to conclusion cannot be assessed as reasonable or unreasonable by a reviewing court.
This is where the procedural fairness doctrine from Week 8 and the standard of review doctrine from this week reinforce each other rather than operating independently.
established a context-sensitive duty to give reasons where the stakes for the individual are significant.

made the quality of those reasons the central criterion of substantive reasonableness. Together, they create a layered accountability structure: a decision-maker must give reasons (procedural fairness), and those reasons must actually justify the decision (substantive reasonableness). A decision that technically produces a reasons document but fills it with bare conclusions satisfies neither requirement.
framework is sometimes described as a deferential standard that keeps courts out of administrative decision-making.
illustrates that this description is incomplete.

defers to the decision-maker's expertise and mandate — it does not defer to inadequate reasoning. The more specialized and expert the tribunal, the more its substantive judgments should be respected; but expert institutions must
. An HRTO that dismisses a human rights complaint without explaining why is not being deferred to under
— it is being sent back to try again.
Cases referenced: Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 | Burland v Precise ParkLink Inc, 2026 ONSC 1587
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 (CanLII)
HRTO 2025 Case Law in Review — Ontario Bar Association

entries.title: Is the OPGT an Administrative Decision-Maker?
author: Fukun
entries.body:
---
# Is the OPGT an Administrative Decision-Maker?
TL;DR
The Office of the Public Guardian and Trustee (OPGT) is a provincial statutory official who makes legally binding decisions about the property and personal care of vulnerable Ontarians — people who are mentally incapable and have no one to act for them. Yes, the OPGT is an administrative decision-maker: it is created by statute, exercises compulsory public authority over some of the most consequential decisions in a person's life, and its decisions are subject to judicial oversight.

---
## Why this question matters
Most administrative law discussions focus on familiar regulatory bodies — licensing agencies, immigration boards, employment tribunals. But some of the most consequential administrative decisions happen in a far less visible place: the management of a person's financial affairs and personal care when they can no longer make decisions for themselves.
Ontario's OPGT is the government's answer to a hard governance problem: who decides for the person who cannot decide for themselves, when there is no family member or trusted person to step in?
The answer turns out to be a statutory office of the Crown — one that exercises powers over property, finances, and medical treatment affecting thousands of Ontarians every year. It is worth understanding what kind of legal actor it is, and what that means for the people subject to its authority.

---
## 1. What is the OPGT?
The Office of the Public Guardian and Trustee is a corporation sole — a legal entity that exists independently of the specific individual holding the office, vested in the Attorney General of Ontario.
Its primary enabling statute is the Substitute Decisions Act, 1992, SO 1992, c 30, which governs who may make decisions about property and personal care on behalf of mentally incapable adults in Ontario.
The OPGT also draws authority from:
- Children's Law Reform Act — acting as litigation guardian for children in court proceedings
- Estates Act — administering estates where no executor or administrator is available
- Mental Health Act — financial management of patients in psychiatric facilities
- Charities Accounting Act — oversight of charitable property

---
## 2. What decisions does the OPGT make?
The OPGT makes binding decisions in three main domains.
### A. Statutory property guardianship (SDA, ss.15–22)
When an adult is found mentally incapable of managing property — and has no valid power of attorney and no court-appointed guardian — the OPGT automatically becomes their statutory guardian of property by operation of statute. No court order is needed. No application is required.
As statutory guardian, OPGT:
- Manages bank accounts, investments, pensions, and real estate
- Pays bills, collects income, and files tax returns
- Makes financial decisions in the person's "best interests"
- Must prepare and implement a formal management plan (SDA, s.32)
### B. Personal care decisions (SDA, s.27 and Health Care Consent Act, 1996)
In urgent situations where a mentally incapable person's personal care is at risk and no substitute decision-maker is available, the OPGT may authorize personal care decisions — including medical treatment.
This is the most intimate form of administrative power: a government official deciding what medical treatment another person receives. The Health Care Consent Act, 1996 provides the framework, with the Consent and Capacity Board as the appellate review body.
### C. Litigation guardianship
The OPGT acts as litigation guardian for parties under a legal disability — including minors and mentally incapable adults — who are parties to court proceedings and have no suitable private representative available.

---
## 3. Is the OPGT an administrative decision-maker?
Yes, unambiguously. Applying the standard definition:
- ✅ Delegated authority? Yes — the Substitute Decisions Act, 1992 expressly creates the OPGT and vests statutory guardianship powers in the office. Authority flows directly from the legislature, not from contract or private arrangement.
- ✅ Affects rights and interests? Profoundly. OPGT decisions control another person's bank accounts, investments, real property, and medical treatment. Few administrative decisions have more direct and intimate impact on an individual's daily life.
- ✅ Public in character? Yes — the OPGT is a Crown corporation sole, performing a mandatory statutory function assigned by the province. It does not contract with its "clients"; it is assigned jurisdiction by operation of law.
The OPGT is not a licensing or regulatory body in the traditional sense — but the definition of ADM is not limited to those forms. Any body exercising delegated statutory authority that affects the rights and interests of individuals qualifies.

---
## 4. Applying the Wall test
In Highwood Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v Wall, 2018 SCC 26, the SCC confirmed that judicial review is available only when (1) there is an exercise of state authority and (2) that exercise is of sufficiently public character.
State authority? The OPGT's powers arise entirely from statute — there is no contract, no voluntary submission, no private arrangement. The OPGT becomes a person's property guardian by operation of the Substitute Decisions Act alone. This is state authority in its most direct form.
Sufficiently public character? Using the Air Canada v Toronto Port Authority contextual factors:
- OPGT's decisions are entirely shaped by statute, not private discretion
- It exercises compulsory powers — the affected person has not chosen the OPGT and cannot dismiss it
- It is accountable to the Attorney General and through the AG to the legislature
- Its mandate — protecting vulnerable citizens who cannot protect themselves — is paradigmatically public in dimension
Both Wall criteria are clearly satisfied. OPGT decisions are subject to judicial review.

---
## 5. Accountability and oversight
Unlike a private trustee — accountable through common law fiduciary duties to a court — the OPGT's accountability operates through multiple layers:

---
## 6. The deeper tension: power without agency
What makes the OPGT uniquely significant in administrative law is not just that it is an ADM — it is that its clients are, by definition, people who cannot advocate for themselves.
The procedural fairness protections that Baker and Vavilov build presuppose a party who can receive notice, participate in a process, and respond to proposed decisions. When the person whose rights are affected lacks legal capacity to participate, the duty of fairness takes on a different character:
- Who represents the person's genuine interests, as distinct from their formal legal interests?
- How does an ADM prevent drift from "best interests" to institutional convenience or resource constraints?
- Does the duty of fairness require the OPGT to seek out and give weight to the incapable person's expressed wishes — even wishes that predate incapacity?
These are not hypothetical concerns. They are live legal and ethical tensions in substitute decision-making law, and they point to a fundamental challenge in administrative law: how to protect procedural fairness when the affected party cannot enforce it themselves.

---
## Key takeaways
1. The OPGT is an ADM: a Crown corporation sole exercising delegated statutory authority over some of the most intimate decisions in a person's life.
1. Its jurisdiction is automatic — the OPGT becomes property guardian by operation of statute, without a court order or the incapable person's consent.
1. Both Wall criteria are met: OPGT decisions involve direct state authority and are paradigmatically public in character.
1. Accountability is multi-layered: management plans, the Consent and Capacity Board, Superior Court review, and judicial review all apply.
1. The deepest tension is substantive: ensuring that "best interest" substituted decisions genuinely serve the incapable person — not institutional efficiency or administrative convenience.

---
## Sources and further reading
### Legislation
- Substitute Decisions Act, 1992, SO 1992, c 30
- Health Care Consent Act, 1996
- Mental Health Act (Ontario)
- Children's Law Reform Act (Ontario)
- Estates Act (Ontario)
### Key cases
- Highwood Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v Wall, 2018 SCC 26 — threshold test for judicial review
- Baker v Canada, 1999 SCC 40 — procedural fairness and ADM obligations
- Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 — standard of review
### Further reading
- Office of the Public Guardian and Trustee — Ontario
- Consent and Capacity Board — Tribunals Ontario
- Substitute Decisions Act Guide — Government of Ontario

entries.title: Week 9 Reflection:  Does Zoom Create a Reasonable Apprehension of Bias
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of March 10, 2026
This week the course covered the rule against bias — one of the oldest principles in natural justice. The doctrine draws two distinctions: actual bias, meaning a decision-maker who is genuinely partial, and reasonable apprehension of bias, which arises when the circumstances viewed by a well-informed observer raise a legitimate concern about impartiality even if the decision-maker is in fact neutral. The operative test comes from Baker v Canada [1999] 2 SCR 817 para 46, citing Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board [1978] 1 SCR 369: would an informed person, viewing the matter realistically and practically and having thought it through, have a reasonable apprehension that the decision-maker would not decide fairly? The test is objective — it asks about the appearance of the process to a hypothetical reasonable observer, not about the adjudicator's subjective state. A finding of reasonable apprehension of bias typically results in the decision being set aside regardless of whether the outcome was correct.
Since 2020, most Ontario administrative tribunals have moved to remote hearings and have largely stayed there. The LTB, LAT, HRTO, and Consent and Capacity Board all conduct a significant proportion of their hearings over Zoom. Tribunals Ontario's remote hearings framework describes this as an access improvement — no travel, no time off work, no childcare logistics. Disability rights advocates have broadly supported remote access. But reading this week's material on bias alongside the reality of how administrative adjudication now works raised a question the doctrine has not fully addressed: does a Zoom hearing change the appearance of impartiality in ways that could, in some circumstances, satisfy the reasonable apprehension test?
Applying the Baker para 46 test to a remote hearing requires thinking carefully about what the "informed observer" is actually watching. In a physical hearing room, signals of engagement or disengagement are visible to all parties: an adjudicator who is looking out the window, who leans forward when one party speaks but visibly disengages when the other does, or who makes impatient comments during one side's submissions sends observable signals. On Zoom, some of those signals are hidden — a camera-off adjudicator is simply invisible — and others are amplified or displaced into different channels. Pre-hearing written communications in case management platforms, for example, can document preliminary views in ways that in-person case management never would have. An adjudicator who records a view on the merits before hearing submissions, in writing, creates a discoverable record that might not have existed before.
The conclusion the Baker test supports is that Zoom does not automatically create a reasonable apprehension of bias, but specific circumstances in a remote hearing can satisfy the test just as they would in person. A camera-off adjudicator who engages actively through questions, who distributes hearing time fairly, and who gives no other adverse signal probably does not create a reasonable apprehension — the informed observer would see a process that, however impersonal, appears impartial. But an adjudicator whose written pre-hearing notes express a preliminary view on the merits of one party's position, or whose audio reveals active engagement during one party's submissions and silence during the other's, may well cross the threshold. The test is about appearance, not modality. What Zoom changes is the set of observable signals available to the informed observer — which means the same analysis applies, but through a different lens.
The harder institutional question is whether remote hearings as a system affect the perceived legitimacy of adjudication in ways the individual bias test doesn't capture. The physical hearing room carries social meaning that Zoom does not replicate — the shared public space, the visible formality of the process, the experience of being heard before a decision-maker who is present in the same room. Whether the loss of that social dimension constitutes a fairness problem that the bias doctrine should address, or whether it is simply a change in form that procedural fairness law is not designed to regulate, is a question I don't think the existing case law has resolved.
Cases referenced: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817 | Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369

---
Sources
- Tribunals Ontario — Remote Hearings
- Baker v Canada, 1999 SCC 40 (CanLII)

entries.title: Why did Landlord Tenant Board succeed s.96 limitation?
author: Fukun
entries.body:
---
# Why Did the Landlord Tenant Board Survive the s.96 Challenge?
TL;DR
The modern Landlord and Tenant Board (LTB) survived its constitutional challenge under s.96 of the Constitution Act, 1867 because (1) landlord-tenant jurisdiction was historically shared — not exclusively Superior Court — and (2) the LTB's adjudicative powers are genuinely incidental to a comprehensive housing policy scheme. An earlier version of the body (the 1981 Residential Tenancies Commission) was struck down for doing exactly what the LTB now avoids: wholesale replacement of court jurisdiction with no broader purpose.
## Why s.96 matters for administrative tribunals
Section 96 of the Constitution Act, 1867 vests the appointment of Superior Court judges in the federal government. The Supreme Court has interpreted this as an implied constitutional guarantee: provincial legislatures cannot strip Superior Courts of their core judicial functions and hand them to administrative bodies.
But this creates a deep tension. Modern governance requires administrative tribunals to make quasi-judicial decisions,adjudicating disputes between landlords and tenants, employers and employees, immigration applicants and the Crown. If s.96 prohibited all such delegation, the administrative state could not function.
The courts' solution is a structured test that separates permissible delegation from unconstitutional court-stripping.
## 1. The constitutional framework: the three-step test
The governing framework emerged from [Reference re Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, [1981] 1 SCR 714](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1981/1981canscc20/1981canscc20.html) and was refined in subsequent cases. Courts ask three questions in sequence:
If Step 1 is "no" — the jurisdiction was never exclusively Superior Court — there is no s.96 problem at all. If Steps 1 and 2 are both "yes" but Step 3 is also "yes" (the adjudication is truly incidental to a broader scheme), the delegation is constitutional.
The critical insight: s.96 protects the core of Superior Court jurisdiction, not every task courts happened to perform in 1867.
## 2. Why the 1981 Commission failed
In the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act Reference, the Supreme Court struck down the Ontario Residential Tenancy Commission.
The Commission had been given virtually exclusive jurisdiction over landlord-tenant disputes — including the power to order eviction. The Court found:
- Step 1  — Eviction orders were historically within the exclusive jurisdiction of Superior Courts at Confederation.
- Step 2 — The Commission's adjudicative process was judicial in character: adversarial, rights-determining, binding.
- Step 3 (fails the test) — The Commission's adjudicative functions were not incidental to a broader scheme. Adjudication was the scheme. It was, in Justice Dickson's words, "a wholesale removal of jurisdiction" from Superior Courts — exactly what s.96 prohibits.
The body existed to replace courts, not to carry out a wider regulatory purpose. That is the constitutional defect.
## 3. Why the modern LTB passes
The modern LTB, established under the Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (Ontario), was carefully structured to avoid repeating this failure.
### A. Historically shared jurisdiction (Step 1 reassessed)
Later jurisprudence — particularly [Reference re Residential Tenancies Act (Nova Scotia), [1996] 1 SCR 186](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1996/1996canscc15/1996canscc15.html) — confirmed that many landlord-tenant matters were never exclusively within Superior Court jurisdiction at Confederation. County courts, magistrates' courts, and other inferior bodies handled landlord-tenant disputes widely in 1867. Where the historical jurisdiction was shared, Step 1 yields "no" — and there is no s.96 problem for those matters.
### B. Genuinely incidental adjudication (Step 3)
The LTB operates within a comprehensive legislative scheme with objectives far exceeding eviction adjudication:
- Balancing the rights and obligations of landlords and tenants
- Promoting access to safe and affordable housing
- Providing mediation and alternative dispute resolution
- Regulating rental housing conditions and standards
- Establishing coherent landlord-tenant policy
The LTB's power to issue eviction orders is subsidiary and incidental to these broader goals — the precise situation Step 3 permits. Eviction authority serves the scheme; it does not constitute the scheme.
### C. Judicial oversight preserved
The LTB is not a shadow court — it is subject to judicial review by the Divisional Court (Ontario Superior Court of Justice). The courts remain the ultimate guarantors of legality. This ongoing oversight relationship is what distinguishes an administrative body from an unconstitutional court-replacement.
## 4. The broader principle from MacMillan Bloedel
In [MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1995/1995canscc100/1995canscc100.html), the SCC drew a critical distinction: s.96 protects the core jurisdiction of Superior Courts — the inherent supervisory jurisdiction over lower courts and the fundamental rights of individuals — not every function Superior Courts happened to exercise in 1867.
Administrative bodies may validly adjudicate disputes, award remedies, and make binding orders — provided:
1. The jurisdiction was historically shared (not exclusively Superior Court), or
1. The judicial function is genuinely incidental to a broader scheme, and
1. The body remains subject to judicial oversight through JR.
The LTB satisfies all three. The 1981 Commission failed on the second and third.
## Key takeaways
1. Section 96 creates an implied protection for the core of Superior Court jurisdiction — provinces cannot wholesale transfer it to administrative bodies.
1. The three-step test asks: is the jurisdiction historically exclusive, judicial in character, and not incidental to a broader scheme?
1. The 1981 Commission failed because adjudication was its primary and only purpose — a direct court-replacement with no regulatory surround.
1. The modern LTB passes because: much of its jurisdiction was historically shared; its adjudicative functions are genuinely incidental to a comprehensive housing policy scheme; and it remains subject to judicial review.
1. The trend is toward generosity: courts have recognized that a functional administrative state requires some overlap between ADM powers and traditional court functions — provided judicial oversight remains intact.
## Sources and further reading
### Key cases
- [Reference re Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, [1981] 1 SCR 714](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1981/1981canscc20/1981canscc20.html) — original commission struck down
- [Reference re Residential Tenancies Act (Nova Scotia), [1996] 1 SCR 186](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1996/1996canscc15/1996canscc15.html) — updated framework; historical shared jurisdiction
- [MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1995/1995canscc100/1995canscc100.html) — core jurisdiction concept
### Legislation
- Constitution Act, 1867, s.96
- Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (Ontario)
### Further reading
- Landlord and Tenant Board — Tribunals Ontario

entries.title: Week 8: How Did We Get a Duty of Fairness, and What Does It Now Require?
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of March 3, 2026
This week traced the evolution of procedural fairness in administrative law across more than 150 years of cases. The story is one of expansion: from a narrow common law principle limited to "judicial" functions, through a series of SCC decisions that dismantled the category-based approach and replaced it with a contextual interest-based standard, to Baker v Canada [1999] 2 SCR 817's five-factor synthesis, and finally to Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz (2022 SCC 29), which extended the fairness inquiry to the duration of proceedings. The central lesson was how the doctrine moved from asking "what type of function is this?" to asking "what is at stake for this person, and what process does that demand?"
Abrametz is both course material and a concrete recent decision worth understanding precisely. Peter Abrametz was a Saskatchewan lawyer found guilty of conduct unbecoming, including making high-interest loans to vulnerable clients. Disciplinary proceedings commenced in 2012 and concluded in 2019 — a 71-month span. The Saskatchewan Court of Appeal stayed his disbarment, finding the delay constituted an abuse of process. The SCC reversed 8-1: Justice Rowe, writing for the majority, held that the delay was long but not inordinate given the complexity of the matter and Abrametz's own contribution to the timeline. Administrative law commentators noted that the significance of the case lies not in the outcome for Abrametz — he lost — but in the two-part test the majority articulated: delay constitutes an abuse of process where it is inordinate in all the circumstances, and where it causes significant prejudice to the person subject to it. Both elements must be present; the threshold is demanding.
Understanding why Abrametz looks the way it does requires following the evolutionary arc the week covered. The starting point is Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works (1863) 143 ER 414, where an English court held that even a statute silent on procedure did not eliminate the common law hearing right — "no man shall be condemned unheard." For a long time, courts confined this to "judicial" functions and refused to apply it to "administrative" ones, producing inconsistent results across provinces and decision-making bodies. Ridge v Baldwin [1964] AC 40 began the shift: Lord Reid held the right to a hearing depended on whether rights or interests were affected, not on whether the function was formally judicial. The SCC followed with Nicholson v Haldimand-Norfolk Regional Police Commissioners [1979] 1 SCR 311, which introduced a freestanding "duty of fairness" not dependent on classifying the function as quasi-judicial, and then Martineau v Matsqui Institution [1980] 1 SCR 602 and Cardinal v Director of Kent Institution [1985] 2 SCR 643, which made it a general common law rule. Knight v Indian Head School Division [1990] 1 SCR 653 added that the content of the duty varies on a spectrum — minimal process for broad policy decisions affecting the public, more robust process for individualized decisions affecting named people's rights.
Baker synthesized all of this into five factors: nature of the decision, statutory scheme, importance to the individual, legitimate expectations, and the agency's own procedural choices. The five factors do not produce a formula, but they give structure to the judgment. Applied to a serious AGCO licence revocation — a named individual, significant economic consequences, a statutory scheme that contemplates adjudication — the Baker factors would require meaningful notice, an opportunity to respond, and written reasons. Applied to a broad IRCC policy announcement affecting all international students in a given category, the duty is much thinner.
Abrametz extended the inquiry further: it is not only the content of the hearing steps that counts, but the time in which they unfold. Delay that is inordinate and causes significant prejudice — reputational harm, professional uncertainty, prolonged anxiety — is itself a fairness violation. For international students navigating immigration proceedings that can span years, this matters. An immigration appeal, a refugee claim, or a permit refusal under judicial review can impose the same kinds of prejudice that Abrametz suffered: precarious status, inability to plan, restricted rights. Abrametz confirms that delay is a cognizable legal wrong, not just an administrative inconvenience — though the threshold of inordinate delay plus significant prejudice is high enough that most delays will not cross it. What the case establishes, at minimum, is that duration is a dimension of fairness the doctrine now formally recognizes.
Cases referenced: Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz, 2022 SCC 29 | Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works (1863) 143 ER 414 | Ridge v Baldwin, [1964] AC 40 | Nicholson v Haldimand-Norfolk Regional Police Commissioners, [1979] 1 SCR 311 | Martineau v Matsqui Institution, [1980] 1 SCR 602 | Cardinal v Director of Kent Institution, [1985] 2 SCR 643 | Knight v Indian Head School Division, [1990] 1 SCR 653 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817

---
Sources
- Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz, 2022 SCC 29 (Supreme Court of Canada)
- Analysis of Abrametz: Procedural Fairness, Undue Delay and Remedial Flexibility (Administrative Law Matters)
- Supreme Court revisits issue of delay in administrative proceedings (HRLSC)
- Delayed Disciplinary Proceedings Not an Abuse of Process, SCC Rules (MLT Aikins)

entries.title: Is Service Ontario an admin decision maker?
author: Fukun
entries.body:
---
# Is ServiceOntario a “Public Actor”?
### A Practical Administrative Law Case Study
TL;DR
## Why this question matters
Everyone in Ontario has used ServiceOntario for essential services (driver’s licence, health card, vehicle registration, etc.).
Because these services are foundational, many people assume ServiceOntario is purely “public.”
But administrative law asks a deeper question:
Is the actor legally public in function, authority, and accountability?
## What triggered this inquiry
During a recent visit, I noticed a sign stating that the branch was operated by a private corporate agency under contract with the Ontario government.
That raises a core legal puzzle:
Can a privately operated location still be a public actor for administrative law purposes?
## 1) ServiceOntario’s legal structure (hybrid model)
ServiceOntario sits within the Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement.
Its delivery model is mixed:
- Some locations are directly government-operated.
- Many are operated by private businesses under contract.
Key point: these operators are not standard franchises in the legal sense.
The Arthur Wishart Act (Franchise Disclosure), 2000 excludes certain Crown service-contract arrangements from “franchise” treatment.
## 2) Administrative law framework
### A. Nature of function test
ServiceOntario performs statutory, governmental functions:
- issuing government identification,
- registering vehicles,
- administering health-card processes,
- processing vital-statistics services.
These are public functions even if frontline delivery is contracted out.
### B. Source of authority
Whether public employee or private operator, authority flows from statute.
The decision-maker is applying provincial legal criteria, not private business policy.
That strongly suggests administrative law principles still apply.
### C. Public-private boundary
Outsourcing delivery does not automatically privatize accountability.
If a contractor is implementing a statutory scheme, courts may still treat those acts as public in substance.
Substance over form is the central principle here.
## 3) Practical challenge: How do you contest a bad decision?
Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act (Ontario), certain statutory decisions are reviewable.
But the hybrid model complicates litigation strategy:
- Who is the proper respondent: Ministry, contractor, or both?
- Which legal frame applies: public law, contract, or mixed?
- Can mandamus-style remedies run effectively against private operators?
- How should contractor action be attributed to government?
## 4) Alternative pathways beyond JR
Depending on facts, possible avenues include:
- Internal administrative reconsideration
- Ontario Ombudsman complaint
- Human rights complaint (if discrimination is involved)
- Civil action (e.g., negligence-related loss)
## 5) Structural gap
Ontario’s framework has not fully clarified this model.
Compared with tribunal systems under the SPPA, there is limited direct guidance on:
- procedural fairness at initial service points,
- responsibility split between contractor and Ministry,
- review route and standards.
## Key takeaways
1. Public function remains public even when delivery is contracted out.
1. Accountability should track power, not organizational form.
1. Residents need clear challenge pathways when legal rights are affected.
1. Hybrid governance is growing, and doctrine must keep pace.
## Conclusion
ServiceOntario’s functions are public in nature and statutory in source.
The contract model should not, in principle, shield decisions from administrative oversight.
Yet in practice, the hybrid structure creates uncertainty for affected individuals.
ServiceOntario is therefore a useful lens for a broader administrative law challenge:
how to preserve accountability as governments modernize service delivery.

---
## Sources and further reading
### Legislation
- Public Service of Ontario Act, 2006
- Statutory Powers Procedure Act
- Judicial Review Procedure Act
- Arthur Wishart Act (Franchise Disclosure), 2000
### Public sources
- Government of Ontario: private ServiceOntario centre information
- Public references documenting ServiceOntario’s 2006 launch and hybrid model
### Concepts
- Procedural fairness (Baker framework)
- Standards of review
- Public-function analysis for private entities
- Scope and availability of judicial review

entries.title: Week 7: Legitimate Expectations and the PGWP Policy Shift
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of February 24, 2026
This week covered the doctrine of legitimate expectations — the principle that, in certain circumstances, a person who relies on a clear government representation has a legal entitlement to procedural protection before that representation is resiled from. Canadian law draws a firm line: legitimate expectations is a procedural doctrine only. A successful claim gives the applicant a right to a fair process — notice, an opportunity to be heard — before a policy changes. It does not guarantee any substantive outcome. The three central cases were Mavi v Canada (2011 SCC 30), which tied the doctrine to formal undertakings and confirmed the procedural-only limit; Agraira v Canada (2013 SCC 36), which extended it to published ministerial guidelines where sufficiently clear and consistent; and Baker v Canada [1999] 2 SCR 817, which emphasized that context and the degree of individual reliance matter in determining what procedural protection is owed. The doctrine's threshold — a representation that is clear, unambiguous, and unqualified — was the central point of discussion.
On September 18, 2024, IRCC announced significant changes to Post-Graduation Work Permit eligibility, introducing a new requirement that PGWP applicants' programs align with specific labour market fields — STEM, healthcare, agriculture, trades, and transportation. Hospitality, social services, and the arts were excluded. The transition protection was narrow: students who had applied for their initial study permit before November 1, 2024 were exempt; those already enrolled but who had not yet submitted that application were not. As Green and Spiegel summarized, students who had chosen specific programs precisely because IRCC's materials indicated those programs would qualify for a PGWP found themselves subject to rules they had no way to anticipate. Protests followed across Canada.
Applying the week's doctrine to these facts produces a concrete answer, even if it is not a satisfying one. The legitimate expectations analysis starts with the threshold requirement: was IRCC's published PGWP guidance a representation that was clear, unambiguous, and unqualified? The answer is almost certainly no. IRCC's web publications on PGWP eligibility have consistently included qualifications — rules are subject to change, programs must continue to meet requirements, no binding commitment is made. Under Mavi, the doctrine requires something closer to a formal undertaking made to an identified party with specific legal content. A general eligibility page with caveats does not meet that standard. Under Agraira, a published guideline might generate expectations if it is sufficiently clear and consistently applied — but the caveats in IRCC's publications were precisely designed to preserve the government's flexibility to change the rules, which takes the PGWP materials below the Agraira threshold.
Even if the threshold were met, the procedural-only limit would significantly constrain what students could obtain. A successful legitimate expectations claim would have required IRCC to give affected students notice of the proposed change and a meaningful opportunity to comment before it took effect — not a right to have the old rules applied to their applications. If IRCC had conducted that process and then still adopted the new field-of-study requirement, the legal outcome for students would be identical. The doctrine gives access to process, not to a result.
The honest conclusion is that the legitimate expectations doctrine, as currently defined in Canadian law, provides limited protection against the kind of harm the 2024 PGWP changes caused. Students who structured significant financial and immigration decisions around government-published eligibility information received no legally enforceable procedural protection when those rules changed. The gap between the harm experienced and the legal remedy available reflects a deliberate doctrinal choice — to preserve government flexibility to change policy — rather than an oversight. Whether that balance is correctly struck is a policy question the courts have consistently declined to revisit.
Cases referenced: Mavi v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2011 SCC 30 | Agraira v Canada (Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness), 2013 SCC 36 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817

---
Sources
- IRCC announces tighter cap for study permits and changes to PGWP eligibility (CIC News, Sept 2024)
- Post-graduation work permit: Field of study requirement (Canada.ca)
- What You Need to Know About the New PGWP Requirements (Green and Spiegel)
- Indian students protest PGWP rules in Canada (The PIE News)

entries.title: Internal Guidelines: tension between jurisprudence towards soft law for ADMs
author: Fukun
entries.body:
# Internal Guidelines: When ADM "Soft Law" Collides with the Rule of Law
TL;DR
Administrative decision-makers routinely develop internal guidelines, manuals, and policies — what scholars call "soft law" — to bring consistency to their discretionary decisions. These instruments are legally powerful but democratically unrecognized. They create three live tensions in administrative law: between consistency and fettering of discretion, between institutional coherence and individual justice, and between legal certainty and democratic accountability.

---
## What triggered this observation
IRCC (Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada) publishes hundreds of pages of internal operational guidelines on its website. Parole boards maintain internal policy directives. The AGCO issues compliance guidelines for licensees. The Canada Revenue Agency has interpretation bulletins.
These documents look like law — they are detailed, authoritative, and routinely followed by decision-makers — but they are not statutes and they are not regulations. They were not enacted by the legislature. They are not subject to parliamentary scrutiny. They can be changed overnight.
And yet they govern tens of thousands of decisions every year, affecting immigration applicants, parolees, licensees, and taxpayers.

---
## What is "soft law" in the administrative context?
Administrative "soft law" refers to rules, policies, guidelines, directives, and manuals that:
- Are produced by an ADM or its enabling ministry
- Structure how the ADM exercises its statutory discretion
- Are not formally enacted as regulations under the Regulations Act or its federal equivalent
- Do not have the force of law in the same sense as a statute or regulation
Common examples:
- IRCC Operational Manuals (governing H&C applications, refugee proceedings, and more)
- Parole Board of Canada Policy Manual
- AGCO compliance guidelines for alcohol and cannabis licensees
- CBSA enforcement bulletins
- CRTC policy positions and frameworks

---
## Tension 1: Consistency vs. Fettering of Discretion
The strongest argument for internal guidelines is that they promote consistency. Decision-makers facing materially identical facts should reach materially similar outcomes. Consistency is itself a value of the rule of law.
But consistency becomes unlawful when a decision-maker applies a guideline rigidly, without genuinely considering whether individual circumstances warrant a different result. This is the doctrine of fettering of discretion.
In [Maple Lodge Farms v Canada, [1982] 2 SCR 2](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1982/1982canscc97/1982canscc97.html), the Supreme Court confirmed: an ADM that exercises statutory discretion must actually exercise it. An ADM that simply applies a policy as if it were mandatory has surrendered its statutory mandate to the policy's drafters — and that mandate belongs to the ADM alone, by virtue of the enabling statute.
The rule: guidelines may structure discretion; they cannot replace it. The decision-maker must always be open to departing from the guideline when the specific facts justify it.
Fettering is a ground of judicial review — a decision that mechanically applies a policy without genuine engagement with the individual case can be quashed.

---
## Tension 2: Baker and the Legal Weight of Guidelines
In Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 1999 SCC 40, the Supreme Court made a landmark ruling on the legal relevance of internal guidelines.
Baker challenged a deportation order made despite her long residence in Canada and her Canadian-born children. The immigration officer had guidelines that directed attention to "the best interests of the child" — guidelines the officer had plainly not applied. Justice L'Heureux-Dubé held:
1. Guidelines can give rise to legitimate expectations — if an ADM has committed to following a particular process or applying a particular standard, individuals can reasonably expect it will do so.
1. Guidelines inform procedural fairness obligations — what the duty of fairness requires in a given case is assessed partly against what the ADM's own guidelines say it will do.
1. Guidelines do not bind the substantive outcome, but they can be relevant to whether the outcome was reached in a fair and reasonable manner.
Baker thus elevated soft law to a legally significant role — not as binding rules, but as contextual markers of what fairness and reasonable discretion require in practice.

---
## Tension 3: Agraira and the Democratic Deficit
In Agraira v Canada (Minister of Public Safety), 2013 SCC 36, the SCC reviewed a ministerial guideline on "national interest" exemptions for inadmissible permanent residence applicants. The Court held that the Minister's decision had to be assessed against the guideline he had published — the guideline was part of the legitimate framework for reviewing how discretion was exercised.
This is legally significant, but it deepens a structural problem: ministerial guidelines effectively function as law, made without legislative process.
Regulations must go through:
- Drafting and internal review
- Cabinet approval
- Tabling in Parliament (federal) or filing with the Registrar (Ontario)
- Publication in the Canada Gazette or Ontario Gazette
- Parliamentary scrutiny
Internal guidelines go through none of this. A Minister can issue, revise, or withdraw a guideline without public consultation, without parliamentary debate, and without judicial review of the guideline itself (only of the decision made under it).
This is what the course calls the "design" tension in administrative law: the efficiency and expertise of the administrative state come at a cost to transparency and democratic legitimacy. Soft law is where that trade-off is sharpest.

---
## Vavilov and the Soft Law Problem
In Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, the SCC emphasized that a reasonable decision must be justified with reference to the legal and factual constraints binding on the decision-maker. Internal guidelines are one such constraint.
Where an ADM departs from its own published guidelines without explanation, that departure may itself be a basis for finding the decision unreasonable — not because the guideline is legally binding, but because unexplained inconsistency with stated policy undermines the transparency and coherence that Vavilov demands. The decision-maker who ignores their own framework cannot claim to be reasoning in good faith.
The flip side: an ADM who rigidly follows a guideline without considering the individual facts has fettered their discretion. Vavilov demands genuine reasoning either way.

---
## A live access-to-justice problem
The soft law problem is not merely academic. It has real consequences for real people:
- A refugee claimant who does not know IRCC's operational guidelines cannot effectively challenge a decision that misapplied them — the guidelines are often the only window into the decision-making framework.
- A cannabis licensee who does not know AGCO's unpublished enforcement priorities cannot know what conduct puts their licence at risk.
- A person subject to a parole directive has no legislative avenue through which to challenge its content — only the decision made under it.
Administrative law partially addresses this through procedural fairness, legitimate expectations, and the Vavilov reasonableness standard. But the democratic deficit created by soft law remains one of the deepest structural tensions in the field.

---
## Key takeaways
1. Soft law (guidelines, policies, manuals) is not legally binding in the same sense as statutes or regulations — but it structurally governs millions of decisions.
1. Fettering doctrine: an ADM must always exercise genuine discretion. Guidelines may structure but cannot replace it (Maple Lodge Farms).
1. Baker: internal guidelines can give rise to legitimate expectations and shape what procedural fairness requires in a given case.
1. Agraira: ministerial guidelines form part of the legitimate framework for assessing whether discretion was reasonably exercised.
1. Vavilov: unexplained departure from published guidelines may render a decision unreasonable for lack of transparency and coherence.
1. The democratic deficit remains: soft law governs at scale without the legislative scrutiny that binds formal regulations.

---

### Key cases

- [Maple Lodge Farms v Canada, [1982] 2 SCR 2](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1982/1982canscc97/1982canscc97.html)
- Agraira v Canada (Minister of Public Safety), 2013 SCC 36
- Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
- Kanthasamy v Canada (Citizenship and Immigration), 2015 SCC 61
### Legislation
- Statutory Instruments Act (Canada)
- Regulations Act (Ontario)
### Further reading

- Parole Board of Canada — Policy Manual

Administrative decision-makers routinely develop internal guidelines, manuals, and policies — what scholars call "soft law" — to bring consistency to their discretionary decisions. These instruments are legally powerful but democratically unrecognized. They create three live tensions in administrative law: between consistency and fettering of discretion, between institutional coherence and individual justice, and between legal certainty and democratic accountability.

IRCC (Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada) publishes hundreds of pages of internal operational guidelines on its website. Parole boards maintain internal policy directives. The AGCO issues compliance guidelines for licensees. The Canada Revenue Agency has interpretation bulletins.

entries.title: Week 5: Did the LTB Actually Survive s. 96, or Just Pass a Low Bar?
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of February 10, 2026
This week covered one of the more unusual constitutional questions in Canadian law: can a provincial legislature take a type of dispute that courts historically decided and hand it to an administrative tribunal instead? Section 96 of the Constitution Act, 1867 gives the federal government the power to appoint judges of superior courts. The SCC has interpreted this to mean that provinces cannot simply strip superior courts of their core jurisdiction and transfer it to a tribunal that the province controls — that would let provinces hollow out federally-appointed courts by delegating their work elsewhere. So when Ontario created the Landlord and Tenant Board to handle evictions and tenancy disputes, there was a genuine constitutional question: was the province doing something it was constitutionally allowed to do?
The answer depends on a three-step test developed in Reference re Residential Tenancies Act, 1979 (Ontario) [1981] 1 SCR 714. First, characterize the power the tribunal is being given. Second, ask whether that power looks like something superior courts exclusively or primarily handled at the time of Confederation in 1867. Third, even if it does, ask whether the tribunal is embedded in a broader regulatory scheme that is distinctly modern — something that wasn't just reproducing what courts used to do, but administering a new kind of statutory regime. The 1981 Ontario scheme failed at step three: the tribunal's power to evict tenants looked too much like what courts had always done, and there was no administrative scheme substantial enough to distinguish it. The legislature had to go back and redesign. The modern LTB, created under the Residential Tenancies Act, 2006, passed all three steps because the new scheme did far more than reproduce common law eviction rules — it created new statutory rights for tenants that had never existed at common law, like the right to maintenance, the right to dispute rent increases, and the right to be free from illegal lockouts. The Reference re Residential Tenancies Act (Nova Scotia) [1996] 1 SCR 186 confirmed the same approach worked for a comparable scheme in another province.
In May 2023, Ontario's Ombudsman released Administrative Justice Delayed, Fairness Denied, a three-year investigation into the LTB. The backlog stood at more than 38,000 cases. Median wait times were seven to eight months for landlord applications; some tenant applications took up to two years. CBC News described the board as "fundamentally failing." The Advocacy Centre for Tenants Ontario documented cases of tenants stuck in units with mould, broken heating, and pest infestations while waiting months for a maintenance hearing. Tribunal Watch Ontario tracked the numbers quarter by quarter. This is where the constitutional framework's limits become visible, because none of what the Ombudsman documented was a constitutional problem.
The three-step test asks one specific question: should this type of dispute be decided by a tribunal at all, or does it belong exclusively to the courts? Once the LTB cleared that test — and it did — the constitution had nothing more to say about whether the LTB needed more adjudicators, faster scheduling, or better technology. Those are resource and policy questions, which are the legislature's to answer. A tenant waiting eight months for a hearing about a mould problem in their apartment is accessing a constitutionally valid institution. The constitution validated the institution's right to exist. It said nothing about the institution's obligation to actually function.
Two additional cases bracket what oversight remains. Crevier v AG Quebec [1981] 2 SCR 220 ensures that if the LTB makes a decision that falls outside its statutory authority — deciding something it was never empowered to decide — superior courts can step in and correct it, no matter what the statute says. MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson [1995] 4 SCR 725 ensures that the legislature cannot take away superior court jurisdiction entirely; courts always retain an irreducible supervisory role. Both cases protect against the LTB overreaching its mandate. Neither requires the LTB to process its current caseload efficiently. For international students in Ontario who rent — often in older housing stock, often unfamiliar with the LTB's procedures, and sometimes reluctant to make complaints for fear of disrupting their housing — the gap between what the constitution guarantees and what the tribunal actually delivers is the part that matters most in practice.
Cases referenced: Reference re Residential Tenancies Act, 1979 (Ontario), [1981] 1 SCR 714 | Reference re Residential Tenancies Act (Nova Scotia), [1996] 1 SCR 186 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220 | MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725

---
Sources
- Administrative Justice Delayed, Fairness Denied — Ombudsman Ontario (May 2023)
- Ontario's Landlord and Tenant Board 'fundamentally failing,' ombudsman finds (CBC News)
- The Landlord and Tenant Board is broken (Advocacy Centre for Tenants Ontario)
- LTB Statement of Concern: The Numbers Speak for Themselves (Tribunal Watch Ontario)

entries.title: Week 4: When Does a Guideline Become Illegal Control?
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of February 3, 2026
This week covered soft law — policies, guidelines, and internal instructions that agencies use to structure discretion without formal rule-making — and introduced the fettering doctrine that governs when those tools cross a legal line. The key tension runs between two cases. Maple Lodge Farms Ltd v Canada [1982] 2 SCR 2 established that mechanically applying a policy without genuine consideration of the individual case is not a lawful exercise of discretion — it is a substitution of the policy for the judgment Parliament intended. But Baker v Canada [1999] 2 SCR 817 treated ministerial guidelines as legitimate and important context for structuring discretion, and Agraira v Canada (2013 SCC 36) confirmed that a reasonable officer would take published guidelines seriously. The doctrinal message is that guidelines are necessary and lawful as a framework, but cannot function as a checklist that replaces case-by-case judgment.
That tension maps directly onto something immigration lawyers have raised publicly for years. In 2022 and 2023, concerns were documented about how IRCC officers assessed the "genuineness of studies" requirement for study permit applications. The operative criteria come from internal Program Delivery Instructions — documents that are not published as regulations and not subject to parliamentary oversight. The International Bar Association has noted that "genuineness" refusals are among the most common and hardest to challenge, often arriving as boilerplate with no applicant-specific reasoning. Lawyers have used access-to-information requests and procedural fairness letter processes to reconstruct which criteria were actually applied — sometimes finding that officers appeared to be working through a checklist of indicators (travel history, financial ties, family circumstances) rather than engaging with the individual file.
This is the Maple Lodge Farms problem applied to immigration. The PDI provides a structured framework; the officer works through it; a refusal is issued. The legal question is whether the officer used the framework as a tool for judgment or as a substitute for judgment. A refusal that identifies that the applicant "has not demonstrated sufficient ties to their home country" without connecting that finding to any specific evidence in the file looks more like the latter. The challenge, which the cases do not fully resolve, is that from the outside a thoughtful application of the framework and a mechanical one can produce identical output. Both cite the same criteria; both arrive at the same result. The fettering problem is partly an evidentiary one.
Vavilov (2019 SCC 65) is where the doctrine provides a practical mechanism. Reasonableness review under Vavilov requires decision-makers to produce reasons that actually grapple with the key considerations in the record — including a demonstration that the guidelines were applied to the specific applicant's circumstances, not just cited. A refusal letter that reproduces PDI language without showing how the officer assessed the particular evidence is more vulnerable to a finding of unreasonableness than one that engages with the file. Vavilov does not eliminate fettering, but it makes it harder to sustain on review by requiring visible reasoning.
The gap that remains is upstream of the individual decision. The PDIs themselves are not easily accessible to applicants. If you don't know the operative framework, you cannot structure your application to address its criteria, and you cannot identify on review whether those criteria were applied thoughtfully. The fettering doctrine addresses the quality of the decision made; the opacity of the framework that shaped it is a separate problem — one that Vavilov and Baker together do not fully answer.
Cases referenced: Maple Lodge Farms Ltd v Canada, [1982] 2 SCR 2 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817 | Agraira v Canada (Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness), 2013 SCC 36 | Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65

---
Sources
- Study permit refusals in Canada (International Bar Association)
- IRCC Procedural Fairness Letter — How to Respond (Canadian Currents Immigration)
- Study Permit Application Refusals (Canadavisa)
- IRCC Ministerial Instructions (Canada.ca)
