entries.title: Week 3: Does Institutional Design Actually Matter?
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of January 27, 2026
This week the course covered the constitutional framework governing how administrative tribunals are designed. Three cases define the boundaries. Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v BC (2001 SCC 52) held that structural features like member tenure and independence are not constitutionally required — they are legislative choices, and courts will not impose their own design preferences on Parliament or the provinces. Crevier v AG Quebec [1981] 2 SCR 220 set the constitutional floor: whatever the legislature designs, it cannot immunize a tribunal's jurisdictional errors from superior court review. MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson [1995] 4 SCR 725 established the outer hard limit: the irreducible core of superior court jurisdiction cannot be fully abolished. Together, these three cases map a spectrum of legislative freedom — wide design latitude under Ocean Port, a jurisdictional floor under Crevier, and an outer constitutional stop under MacMillan Bloedel.
In December 2021, the AGCO suspended and moved to revoke the liquor licence of Zucchinis Cucina, an Oakville restaurant that had admitted unvaccinated dine-in patrons during Ontario's proof-of-vaccination period. It was part of a broader enforcement campaign — the AGCO's COVID-19 pandemic response describes Compliance Officials participating in multi-ministry enforcement sweeps. Some operators complained about the speed of the actions and argued that the AGCO's published progressive enforcement framework was not being consistently followed. The right way to assess those complaints is to run them through the week's constitutional framework.
Start with Ocean Port. The AGCO's structure — a governing Commission setting policy, a Registrar applying it to individual cases, and a separate Licence Appeal Tribunal reviewing challenges — is entirely a product of the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario Act, 2019. Under Ocean Port, all of that is a valid legislative choice. The degree of Commission oversight over the Registrar, the partial separation between policy-setting and adjudication, the tenure conditions of LAT members — these are design decisions the legislature was entitled to make, and a court applying Ocean Port will not second-guess them. An operator who thinks the AGCO is structurally biased has no constitutional argument based on design alone.
Next, Crevier. The jurisdictional floor matters if the Registrar's enforcement exceeded its statutory authority — for example, if it suspended a licence on grounds that the AGCO Act does not actually authorize. If the Registrar acted outside its statutory remit, the LAT and, beyond it, superior courts retain jurisdiction to review that excess, and no privative clause can take that away. On the COVID enforcement facts, the statutory authority question turns on whether violations of public health orders issued under separate legislation were valid grounds for AGCO action under the AGCO Act. That is a genuine legal question, but the AGCO does not appear to have faced a successful jurisdictional challenge on this basis, suggesting courts found the statutory authority was present.
The conclusion from applying all three cases to the Zucchinis Cucina scenario is that the AGCO's design and its COVID enforcement actions were constitutionally sound. Ocean Port protected the design; Crevier preserved judicial oversight; MacMillan Bloedel's outer limit was nowhere near triggered. But the more important takeaway is that constitutional validity does not exhaust the legal inquiry. Law Society of Ontario v AA (2026 ONCA 47), decided just recently, shows that the ONCA will separately ask whether the process in a specific case was adequate to the stakes involved — a Baker-style procedural fairness question that operates entirely independently of whether the tribunal's structure is constitutionally valid. For AGCO operators who received little notice before a suspension, that procedural fairness question is the relevant one, and it is where the real legal dispute lives.
Cases referenced: Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v British Columbia, 2001 SCC 52 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220 | MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725 | Law Society of Ontario v AA, 2026 ONCA 47

---
Sources
- AGCO suspends and moves to revoke the liquor licence of Zucchinis Cucina (AGCO)
- The AGCO's response to the COVID-19 pandemic (AGCO)

Cases referenced: Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v British Columbia, 2001 SCC 52 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220 | MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725 | Law Society of Ontario v AA, 2026 ONCA 47

entries.title: Week 2: What Makes a Decision "Governmental Enough" to Review?
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of January 20, 2026
This week built on last week's question — not just what an administrative decision-maker is, but which ones are actually subject to judicial review. Judicial review is the process by which courts check whether a public body acted within its legal authority and treated people fairly. It is a powerful tool, but it is not available against every organization that makes decisions affecting people's lives. The Wall test (2018 SCC 26) determines when it applies: a body must be exercising public power — authority that flows from the state through legislation and that makes the body governmental in nature. If the body's authority comes from a contract, from consent, or from a purely private arrangement, the courts say: that is a private matter, and private law — not judicial review — is your remedy.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, Ontario condominium corporations issued rules restricting elevator access, banning guests, and in some buildings requiring proof of vaccination just to use shared amenities. Some residents received fines. CBC News reported on a downtown Toronto condo rolling out a vaccine policy for common areas; the Globe and Mail noted that boards were left to set their own rules without provincial guidance. A fined resident asking "can I challenge this in court?" would quickly run into the Wall test. Condo boards get their governance powers from the Condominium Act, but the nature of the relationship is fundamentally private — you bought into a community of property owners, and disputes within that community stay there. Courts treated COVID condo policies as valid exercises of board authority, enforceable through the Condominium Authority Tribunal and the Act's own dispute resolution processes, not through judicial review. The Wall test gave the right answer here.
Before Wall, courts had tried three different approaches to drawing this line, and each produced different results for the same kinds of bodies. One approach focused on where the legal authority came from — does it trace back to a statute? Another looked at a list of factors — government funding, government control, the public character of the function — to determine whether the body was "sufficiently governmental." A third tried to distinguish between bodies exercising statutory powers and bodies exercising private powers even within a regulated industry. The problem was that condo boards, universities, and professional associations could come out differently depending on which approach a judge preferred. Wall replaced all three with one question: is the power the body is exercising a public power? That produced more predictable outcomes, even if the boundary remains genuinely difficult in some cases.
Aga v Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church of Canada St. Mary Cathedral (2021 SCC 22), decided three years after Wall, shows where the clean answer gets uncomfortable. A religious community expelled members from the church. The SCC applied Wall and held the decision was not reviewable — the church's authority over its members comes from religious community and consent, not from the state. Legally, this is correct. Practically, it means that people expelled from a tight-knit religious community, with serious social and personal consequences, have no public law remedy at all. The Wall test delivered clarity; it did not promise justice for every situation it classifies as private.
For me as an international student, the most consequential institution in my daily life is not a government agency — it is my university. Universities are created by provincial statute and receive public funding, but they also have a private, consensual dimension: you chose to enroll, you agreed to the institution's rules. A finding of academic dishonesty can cost me my enrolment and, because my study permit is tied to being a full-time student, can unravel my immigration status in Canada. Whether that disciplinary decision is subject to judicial review or left to the university's internal process depends on exactly the Wall characterization. MacKinnon v University of Guelph (2025 FC 422) shows federal courts are actively grappling with where universities sit. Wall simplified the test. It did not resolve the cases where the line between public and private is genuinely uncertain — and for students in serious disciplinary proceedings, uncertainty about which forum applies is itself a serious problem.
Cases referenced: Wall v Independent Planning Association, 2018 SCC 26 | Aga v Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church of Canada St. Mary Cathedral, 2021 SCC 22 | MacKinnon v University of Guelph, 2025 FC 422

---
Sources
- Toronto condo building brings in vaccine policy for amenities (CBC News)
- In condo land, COVID-19 vaccine rules are hazy (The Globe and Mail)
- Condo COVID-19 Policy Upheld as Enforceable by Court (Lash Condo Law)

entries.title: Week 1: Who Is Actually Making the Decision?
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of January 13, 2026
This week introduced the foundational question of administrative law: what makes a body an "administrative decision-maker" subject to judicial review? The answer the course gave is the source-of-power test — a body exercises public law power when its authority derives from statute, and it is that statutory delegation that triggers administrative law accountability. Roncarelli v Duplessis [1959] SCR 121 is the classic illustration: when a government official used statutory power to revoke a liquor licence as personal retaliation, the SCC held that the exercise of that authority was legally constrained precisely because the power was public. Statutory source equals public accountability.
When I renewed my study permit last year, the first step was not submitting anything to IRCC — it was booking an appointment through VFS Global, a private company contracted by the government to collect biometrics and application documents. You pay a separate service fee to VFS, attend their commercial office, and only then does IRCC receive your materials and make the actual decision. IRCC's own VFS consent form states explicitly: "VFS Global is not an agent of the Government of Canada but is a completely independent entity operating under contract." That sentence matters legally. If something goes wrong at the VFS stage — documents mishandled, biometrics incorrectly recorded — the source-of-power test points to a clear answer: VFS has no statutory authority, it is not an administrative decision-maker, and I have no administrative law remedy against it.
A similar pattern appears with ServiceOntario, which has contracted out vehicle and driver licensing services to private registry agents — pharmacies, private offices, dealerships — for years. The Auditor General of Ontario has flagged the accountability gap this creates: when private operators make errors affecting people's licences, there is no clear public law path to correction because the operators are not statutory decision-makers. These are not hypothetical problems. They affect real people whose licences, permits, and status depend on functions that the government has delegated to private hands.
Applying the source-of-power test to VFS produces a technically correct but practically unsatisfying result. VFS receives documents; IRCC makes the eligibility decision. The statutory power to grant or refuse a study permit lives with IRCC under the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act. VFS exercises no delegated public law power and is therefore, on this analysis, outside administrative law's reach. What this conclusion misses is that VFS is a mandatory gateway to a statutory right — there is no way to submit a study permit application without going through VFS for biometrics. An error at that stage can delay or derail an application with serious downstream consequences for immigration status, employment authorization, and study continuity. The person harmed has no direct administrative law remedy against the body that caused the harm.
The source-of-power test was developed when government acted through its own officials, not through contracted private infrastructure. The Roncarelli principle — that public authority creates public accountability — has not yet been applied to ask who is accountable when public authority is intentionally routed through private architecture. Until the doctrine catches up with the reality of outsourced service delivery, there is a genuine accountability gap that affects people who have no way to avoid it.
Cases referenced: Roncarelli v Duplessis, [1959] SCR 121

---
Sources
- Biometrics — Where to give your fingerprints and photo (IRCC)
- VFS Global Consent Form for Canadian Visa Applications
- Office of the Auditor General of Ontario

entries.title: Travels, Pat Metheny Group, 1984
author: Fukun
entries.body:
While ECM is known for its minimalism aesthetics, Pat Metheny perfectly balanced the minimalism and smooth in his jazz guitar works.
Travels is a piece perfect for serene travelling time, roadtrip or by the ocean. Whenever I play it, it feels breezing through the window of a car.

entries.title: Risky in a risky world
author: Fukun
entries.body:
Risky, Ryuichi Sakamato, 1987
From his early stage of music life, Sakamoto’s NEO GEO is a rather interesting experiment of okinawa folk song and electronic music. Risky features Iggy Pop, with thought provoking lyrics.

entries.title: By the Suzhou River
author: Fukun
entries.body:
By the suzhou river, The Shanghai Restoration Project, 2014
I really love this concept of electronic adaptations of classic songs - so light and ethereal. My favorite is of course “By the Suzhou River.” “We walked and lost our way, wandering along the riverside embankment, not knowing whether the world abandoned us, or we forgot it.” Chen Gexin’s lyrics are truly wonderful. These lines echo that line from Mimik Banka’s“Morning Star”: “In an ordinary corner of this world, I live on indifferently, together with you.” The nimble electronic foundation paired with Zhang Le’s slightly melancholic voice, the collision of lightness and weight creates a buoyant sense of fate.
极喜欢这张电子化经典歌曲的概念，轻盈飘逸，最爱的当然是《苏州河边》，“我们走着迷失了方向，尽在岸堤河边彷徨，不知是世界离弃了我们，还是我们把他遗忘”，陈歌辛的词真好，这几句和表情银行《启明星》里那句“在这世界的普通角落，我满不在乎地活下去，和你一起”异曲同工。灵动的电子乐基底搭配张乐略带怅然的歌声，轻和重冲撞，轻盈的宿命感。

entries.title: Silent Song, Jessica Pratt, 2019
author: Fukun
entries.body: A guitar weaves timeless melodies, with a voice so ethereal it’s almost ghostly. Jessica Pratt’s style is captivating and enchanting, vintage and serene, perfect for listening to while sitting idly in a café—it’s the ultimate alone music.
